hook: A toy fishing harbour of fifty cabins wedged under a road viaduct
facts: Tucked beneath the Corniche Kennedy viaduct in the 7th arrondissement, 2.5 km from the Vieux-Port
Roughly fifty fishermen's cabins and traditional pointu boats ring the basin
Named for 'auffe' (alfa grass), once braided here into rope and nets
w: A toy harbour swallowed by the city: fifty fishermen's cabins and stubby pointu boats packed around a basin barely wider than a swimming pool, the Corniche viaduct arching overhead. Named for the alfa grass once braided into rope and nets here. The Mediterranean, shrunk to pocket size.

hook: Fortified abbey over catacomb crypts and a green-cloaked Black Madonna
facts: Founded in the late Roman period over earlier quarry-cut catacombs and martyrs' tombs
The crypt guards a Black Virgin paraded each Candlemas in a green mantle
Rebuilt with crenellated towers in the 14th century, looking more keep than church
legend: They say the saint Victor was crushed between millstones, his relics drawing pilgrims to the crypt below.
w: Half church, half fortress, crenellated towers glowering over the port. Beneath the nave, catacomb crypts cut into old quarry rock hold martyrs' tombs and a Black Madonna in a green cloak, carried out each Candlemas. Legend says Victor met his end between two millstones. Pilgrims still come for the dark below.

hook: The city's oldest bakery, blessing boat-shaped biscuits since 1781
facts: Operating at 136 rue Sainte since 1781, on the sacred way to Abbaye Saint-Victor
Still bakes in its original round oven; the navette recipe stays secret
Each Candlemas the archbishop comes to bless the oven and the biscuits
legend: The navette's boat shape supposedly recalls the vessel that carried the Saintes-Maries to Provence.
w: Since 1781 the same round oven on the road to Saint-Victor has turned out navettes, hard little boat-shaped biscuits scented with orange blossom, the recipe kept secret for two centuries. Every Candlemas the archbishop walks down to bless the oven. Legend says the shape recalls the boat that bore the saints to Provence.

hook: Puget's pink-stone poorhouse with a domed chapel at its heart
facts: Built 1671-1749 to designs by Marseille-born sculptor-architect Pierre Puget
Three storeys of arcaded galleries enclose an oval-domed Baroque chapel
Once a workhouse for the city's poor; now museums of Mediterranean and African art
w: Behind a blank wall in the Panier hides a poorhouse the locals call a palace: three tiers of pink-stone arcades wrapped around an oval-domed chapel, all by the Marseillais sculptor Puget. For centuries it warehoused the destitute; slum-clearance nearly demolished it. Now it shows Mediterranean art. Grandeur built for the poor.

hook: A vast dead tobacco factory reborn as an arts city
facts: Sprawls across the 45,000 sq m former Seita tobacco works, shut in 1990
Turned over to artists from 1992; studios, theatres, galleries and skatepark
A rooftop terrace overlooks the rail yards and the rooftops of the city
w: The Seita tobacco works rolled cigarettes here until 1990, then died. Rather than raze the 45,000 square metres, the city handed it to artists: now studios, theatres, a bookshop and skatepark sprawl through the sheds, with a rooftop terrace over the rail yards. Industry's carcass, kept warm by paint and noise.

hook: The old wholesale market square turned open-air mural gallery
facts: Was Marseille's wholesale fruit-and-veg market, 'the belly', from about 1860 to 1970
Now a warren of repainted facades, stencils and tags in the 6th arrondissement
Centred on a planted, fountain-set square ringed by bars and record shops
w: Once 'the belly' of Marseille, the wholesale market where lorries dumped fruit until 1970. When the trade moved out, artists moved in, and every wall, staircase and shutter went under spray paint. The planted square fills with bar tables by night. A market square that swapped crates for colour.

hook: The clamorous North African market they call the belly of Marseille
facts: Daily market off La Canebiere in the Noailles quarter, the 1st arrondissement
Stalls heap with spices, olives, herbs and produce from across the Mediterranean
Long the cheapest fresh market in the centre, and the city's most diverse street
w: Step off the Canebiere into Noailles and the noise hits first: vendors crying prices over mounds of spices, mint, olives and dates from every shore of the sea. They call it the belly of Marseille, the cheapest, loudest, most polyglot market in the centre. The whole Mediterranean, sold by the kilo.

hook: A monumental water-castle raised to celebrate the city's first clean water
facts: Built over 30 years to 1869, marking the Canal de Marseille bringing Durance water
Centred on a 10 m cascading chateau d'eau crowned by 'The Triumph of the Durance'
Its colonnaded wings hold the fine-arts and natural-history museums
w: A palace built around a tap. After cholera and thirst, the Durance canal finally brought clean water, and the city marked it with this colonnaded water-castle: a cascade tumbling under bronze bulls and the figure of the river herself. Museums fill the wings. Civic plumbing dressed as triumphal arch.

hook: A wartime blockhouse below Fort Saint-Jean turned memorial
facts: Set in a 1943 German blockhaus at the foot of Fort Saint-Jean, 2nd arrondissement
Recalls the 1943 razing of the Vieux-Port quarter and the deportation of residents
Reopened as the Memorial des Deportations in 2019
w: A squat German blockhouse, poured into the rock below Fort Saint-Jean in 1943 to repel a landing that never came. Inside, it now remembers what happened just behind it: the old port quarter dynamited block by block in 1943, its people rounded up and deported. Concrete built to kill, kept to mourn.

hook: A grand 104-step stair of colonial allegories spilling from the station
facts: Opened 1925, completed 1927; 104 steps falling 15.5 m to Boulevard d'Athenes
Flanked by stone allegories of Marseille's Greek colony and the 'Gate of the Orient'
Bronze groups celebrate the products of Provence and the far-off colonies
w: Arrive by train and the city greets you with a stone cascade: 104 steps tumbling from the station down to the boulevard, lined with allegories of Marseille's Greek founders and the colonial 'Gate of the Orient'. Cut in 1925 when empire flowed through this port. A staircase as imperial billboard, now just a place to sit.

hook: A fortress Louis XIV pointed inward at the rebellious city
facts: Built from 1660 by the Chevalier de Clerville to guard the Vieux-Port entrance
Its guns faced the town, raised to cow Marseille after revolt against the crown
Split by a road into Fort d'Entrecasteaux above and Fort Ganteaume below
w: Louis XIV built two forts at the harbour mouth, and pointed one of them at his own city. Saint-Nicolas trained its guns inward, a stone warning to a Marseille that kept rebelling against the crown. Later cut in half by a road. The upper ward is open again, all bastions and ditches. Royal menace, with sea views.

hook: Pagnol's 'chateau of my mother', bought to build a cinema city
facts: The 19th-century bastide of Marcel Pagnol's 'Le Chateau de ma mere'
Pagnol bought it in 1941 hoping to found a 'cinema city' of stages and workshops
Restored by the city in 2011 as a Maison des cinematographies de la Mediterranee
w: As a boy Pagnol crept along a forbidden wall past this bastide on the walk to his summer holidays; decades later he recognised it, bought it, and called it the chateau of his mother. He dreamed of a cinema city here, with stages and workshops. The scheme failed; the city restored it. Childhood fear turned shrine to film.

hook: The fishermen's pink-stone church perched over the harbour mouth
facts: Provencal Romanesque church in Cap Couronne pink stone, late 12th century
Became the fishermen's parish, the city's fourth, by 1249
Stands on a former fortified mound above the Vieux-Port entrance
w: Low, solid and the colour of rose, built from Cap Couronne stone over a fortified mound where the harbour narrows. For centuries this served the fishermen, the city's fourth parish, blessing the boats below. Its squat Romanesque silhouette reads more like a watchtower than a church. Faith with its back to the wind.

hook: Napoleon III's seafront palace, finished just as his empire fell
facts: Begun 1858 in Second Empire style for Empress Eugenie, on a headland over the port
The emperor never stayed; he died in 1873 and Eugenie gave it to the city
Served as a cholera hospital and tropical-medicine school before becoming an events venue
w: Napoleon III raised this palace on a headland for Eugenie, with the whole bay laid at its feet, then the empire collapsed before he could spend a night in it. Widowed, she handed it to the city, which turned it into a cholera hospital and a tropical-medicine school. Now its lawns are open. A dream house no emperor slept in.

hook: A quiet cove named for legends of secret coining
facts: Small cove off the Corniche Kennedy, near the Anse de Maldorme, 7th arrondissement
Marked 'La monoy' on a 1736 chart and once called the 'valley of silence'
Home to a nautical society since 1910, below the cliff-edge road
legend: Legend says a local notable, Charles de Cazault, minted his own coins in protest and flung them into this sea.
They say counterfeiters' tools were found here, giving the cove its name.
w: A scrap of cove below the Corniche, once called the valley of silence. Legend says a defiant notable, Charles de Cazault, struck his own coins in protest and threw them into the water here; others say forgers' tools turned up on the rocks. The truth is mislaid. The name 'false money' stuck, and so did the quiet.
